source_inputs.use_in_this_artifact: claim ids, causal edges, product definition, trust model, and public surface routing
slide roles, reader questions, required answers, and product terms
proof objects, public takeaway, copy units, and note/viewer routing

artifact_scope.goal: Turn the C1 product claim graph into a six-slide story plan with title, thesis, proof object, visual object, text budget, and demo goal per slide.
artifact_scope.quality_gate: Every slide must answer its reader question with a product claim, proof object, visible result, and note/viewer route while avoiding renderer geometry.

story_arc.one_sentence: A real brief enters a source-bound system, becomes product memory, binds tutorial design moves, generates editable PPT, proves the workflow, then stops at a review gate.
story_arc.public_demo_boundary: This is a story plan for a blocked public demo candidate, not a generated deck or release approval.

slides.story_function: Name the product and make the output concrete before the viewer evaluates design quality.
slides.audience_question: What is Vulca and what concrete product output does this deck demonstrate?
slides.title: A real brief becomes editable PPT proof.
slides.thesis: Vulca is a source-bound system that turns a commercial brief and selected source memory into a code-generated editable PPT deck.

proof_object.why_this_proves_the_claim: The viewer sees the business input and editable output in one path, so the claim is not just a style promise.

must_not_show: decorative hero with no product object

on_canvas_copy.headline: A real brief becomes editable PPT proof.
on_canvas_copy.supporting_line: Commercial brief, curated source memory, and code-generated editable PPT appear as one product path.

speaker_note_or_viewer_route.speaker_note: State that this is not an image-only demo; the product promise is editable PPT with inspectable inputs.

text_budget.density_reason: The cover must be instantly readable and leave the product path to carry proof.

slides.demo_goal: Within one slide, the viewer understands the product category and output.

content_risk.failure_mode: The cover looks like a pretty launch slide but does not explain the product.

handoff_to_scene_compiler.required_scene_question: How can the scene show brief, memory, editable output, and review boundary as connected native objects?

slides.story_function: Show that source material is compiled into product memory before drawing starts.
slides.audience_question: How does Vulca transform raw business and tutorial material into design instructions?
slides.title: Sources become product memory before drawing.
slides.thesis: Allowed source observations are reduced into capability memory, message obligations, and proof objects before any slide is rendered.

proof_object.why_this_proves_the_claim: The slide shows the mechanism that turns source inputs into obligations, so design choices have upstream reasons.

on_canvas_copy.headline: Sources become product memory before drawing.
on_canvas_copy.supporting_line: Allowed source observations become capability memory, message obligations, and proof objects.

speaker_note_or_viewer_route.speaker_note: Explain that tutorials and videos guide decisions only after they are converted into original product memory.

text_budget.density_reason: The setup carries mechanism detail, but raw source evidence stays off-canvas.

slides.demo_goal: The viewer sees that Vulca has an understanding layer, not just a renderer.

content_risk.failure_mode: The slide sounds like vague data magic.

handoff_to_scene_compiler.required_scene_question: How can the scene show source to memory to obligation to proof without becoming a table?

slides.story_function: Differentiate Vulca from prompt-only and image-only alternatives by showing the missing mechanism.
slides.audience_question: Why is this different from prompt-only, image-only, template, design-editor, or office-copilot slide creation?
slides.title: The difference is mechanism, not taste.
slides.thesis: Prompt-only can produce plausible slides, but it lacks source memory, proof routing, mapped design moves, and editable output guarantees.

proof_object.why_this_proves_the_claim: The contrast is a system boundary, not a beauty contest.

on_canvas_copy.headline: The difference is mechanism, not taste.
on_canvas_copy.supporting_line: Vulca binds source memory, proof routing, design moves, and editable output before the deck is claimed.

speaker_note_or_viewer_route.speaker_note: Name prompt-only and image-only paths as missing mechanism, not as inferior style by default.

text_budget.density_reason: The contrast slide needs enough terms to make differentiation objective.

slides.demo_goal: The viewer understands why Vulca is a product workflow rather than a prompt or template variant.

content_risk.failure_mode: The slide becomes a subjective competitor table.

handoff_to_scene_compiler.required_scene_question: How can the scene show the competing route breaking at missing mechanism while Vulca continues?

slides.story_function: Make credibility visible without turning the slide into a compliance report.
slides.audience_question: How do we know the product really used the data and workflow instead of producing another direct deck?
slides.title: Proof stays inspectable without taking over the slide.
slides.thesis: Each public proof marker points to capability binding, contract match, content check, and bad-control failure behind the surface.

proof_object.why_this_proves_the_claim: The slide shows enough proof to trust the workflow while routing raw evidence to the viewer.

on_canvas_copy.headline: Proof stays inspectable without taking over the slide.
on_canvas_copy.supporting_line: Compact proof markers show that the workflow consumed content, contracts, and comparison boundaries.

speaker_note_or_viewer_route.speaker_note: Explain that raw proof stays inspectable off-canvas so the public slide remains readable.

text_budget.density_reason: Trust requires named proof states, but raw detail must stay off-canvas.

slides.demo_goal: The viewer trusts that the deck used the intended data and workflow.

content_risk.failure_mode: The slide turns into an engineering audit dump.

handoff_to_scene_compiler.required_scene_question: How can four proof states be made visible as compact native objects with a viewer pointer?

slides.story_function: Create the strongest remembered product moment around the editable result.
slides.audience_question: What is the strongest product moment the public demo should remember?
slides.title: One editable result owns the product moment.
slides.thesis: The brief, source memory, mapped design moves, code renderer, editable preview, and QA proof converge into one result object.

proof_object.why_this_proves_the_claim: The product moment is a generated editable result with proof attached, not a set of disconnected diagrams.

on_canvas_copy.headline: One editable result owns the product moment.
on_canvas_copy.supporting_line: Design memory, mapped design moves, code generation, and proof converge into one editable PPT preview.

speaker_note_or_viewer_route.speaker_note: Keep this slide cinematic and least report-like; proof should orbit the result, not compete with it.

text_budget.density_reason: The hero result should dominate; copy supports the moment.

slides.demo_goal: The viewer remembers Vulca as an operating loop that produces an editable artifact.

content_risk.failure_mode: The climax is diluted by too many equal proof cards.

handoff_to_scene_compiler.required_scene_question: How can the editable result dominate while proof remains attached and legible?

slides.story_function: Close with the next concrete proof and preserve the release boundary.
slides.audience_question: What should happen next before we claim this product is publishable?
slides.title: The next proof is generated, not claimed.
slides.thesis: The story can feed the next generated deck, but public release waits for visual review, native inspection, source-boundary review, and human approval.

proof_object.why_this_proves_the_claim: The close is credible because it names the remaining proof instead of claiming release.

on_canvas_copy.headline: The next proof is generated, not claimed.
on_canvas_copy.supporting_line: This story becomes input for the next generated deck, then waits for review before release.

speaker_note_or_viewer_route.speaker_note: Name the remaining gate calmly: generated proof, visual review, native inspection, source-boundary review, and human approval.

text_budget.density_reason: The close should be quiet and decisive, not a full process appendix.

slides.demo_goal: The viewer knows the exact next step and does not mistake the content story for release approval.

handoff_to_scene_compiler.required_scene_question: How can the scene show a calm blocked release gate with the next generated proof clearly named?
